question: A PID controller is the backbone of feedback loops in AMO labs. Which statement correctly assigns the role of each term?

options: P sets loop gain and reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error; I integrates the error to zero out the offset and adds phase lag; D responds to the error's rate of change and amplifies noise
P eliminates steady-state error; I provides fast corrections to noise; D sets the overall loop gain and bandwidth
I sets the loop gain and reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error; P integrates the error to zero out the offset; D scales the noise floor
All three terms have the same physical effect; the P, I, and D coefficients are simply three independent gain knobs at three different frequencies
passive.explanation: PID transfer function: $G(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. Loop stability: the Bode criterion requires gain $< 1$ at the phase-crossover frequency. Common tuning: Ziegler-Nichols (increase $K_p$ until oscillation, then set $K_i, K_d$ from the oscillation period). In laser locking: P sets capture range, I corrects drift, D is often omitted (adds noise). Integrator wind-up (integrator saturates when error is large) is a common problem — add anti-windup clamping. Unity-gain bandwidth $f_{\rm UGB}$ must lie below the phase crossover for stability.

active.prompt: Match each PID term to its Laplace-domain action and effect on the loop:

pairs.definition: Gain $K_p \cdot e(t)$; increases loop gain; reduces (but does not zero) steady-state error; can cause oscillation if too large
Integrates error over time; eliminates steady-state offset; adds $-90°$ phase lag; risk of integrator wind-up
Responds to rate of change of error; adds phase lead; damps ringing; amplifies high-frequency noise
Frequency at which loop gain = 1; must lie below phase crossover ($< 180°$ phase shift) for stability; sets correction speed

active.explanation: Practical laser locking: typical PDH servo has UGB $\sim 1$ MHz (limited by cavity pole and EOM bandwidth). For intensity stabilisation: AOM or EOM as actuator; servo UGB $\sim 100$ kHz. For current locking: diode current as actuator, UGB $\sim 1$ MHz. Phase margin $> 45°$ recommended for robust lock. Digital PID (FPGA-based, e.g., Koheron, Vescent D2-125): update rate $\sim 100$ MHz; 16-bit precision; easy parameter tuning.

question: A lock-in amplifier (LIA) extracts a signal at a known reference frequency from a noisy background. What is the key principle?

options: Multiply the input signal by a reference $\cos(\omega_r t + \phi)$ then low-pass filter; only components at $\omega_r$ survive; the noise bandwidth is set by the low-pass time constant $\tau$
Apply a narrow analog bandpass filter centred at $\omega_r$ to the input signal; the filter's $Q$ determines the SNR improvement
Subtract the noise floor measured in a separate channel from the input, then take the magnitude at $\omega_r$ via an FFT
Use a phase-locked loop to track the input frequency and an envelope detector to recover the amplitude at any reference frequency
passive.explanation: Lock-in SNR advantage: $\text{SNR}_{\rm LIA} = \text{SNR}_{\rm direct} \cdot \sqrt{B_{\rm in} / B_{\rm LIA}}$ where $B_{\rm in}$ is the input noise bandwidth and $B_{\rm LIA} = 1/(4\tau)$ is the LIA bandwidth. Example: measuring a 1 nV signal at 10 kHz in 10 Hz detection bandwidth rejects noise from 1 Hz to 100 kHz — $10^4$ bandwidth ratio → 40 dB SNR improvement. Time constant $\tau = 100$ ms → bandwidth $\sim 2.5$ Hz. Dual-phase detection: $X = V\cos\phi$, $Y = V\sin\phi$, $R = \sqrt{X^2+Y^2}$ (amplitude), $\theta = \arctan(Y/X)$ (phase). AMO uses: absorption spectroscopy SNR enhancement; PDH demodulation; noise floor characterisation.

question: Electronic noise in AMO lab circuits limits SNR. Which combination correctly identifies the fundamental noise sources and their frequency dependence?

options: Johnson-Nyquist (thermal) noise: $S_V = 4k_BT R$, white spectrum; shot noise on photocurrent: $S_I = 2eI$, white; $1/f$ (flicker) noise dominates at low frequency; sources add in quadrature
Johnson noise scales as $1/f$ at all temperatures; shot noise is independent of current; $1/f$ noise is white above $\sim 1$ kHz
Shot noise scales as $I^2$; Johnson noise scales as $T^2$; $1/f$ noise is a power-line artefact and disappears in battery-powered circuits
All noise sources have the same spectral shape and amplitude in well-designed analogue circuits
passive.explanation: Johnson noise RMS voltage: $V_n = \sqrt{4k_BT R B}$. At $R = 1$ k$\Omega$, $T = 300$ K, $B = 1$ MHz: $V_n = 4$ nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ (typical op-amp noise floor). Shot noise on photocurrent: at $I = 1$ mA, $I_n = \sqrt{2eIB} = 18$ pA/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$. Standard quantum limit (SQL): photon shot noise sets fundamental sensitivity in homodyne detection — going below it requires squeezed states. Op-amp voltage noise: typical $\sim 1{-}5$ nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ (e.g., LT1028: 0.9 nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$). Ground loops: capacitively-coupled 50/60 Hz pick-up — eliminate by single-point star grounding.

question: EOMs and AOMs are the primary tools for controlling laser frequency, intensity, and phase. Which statement correctly contrasts their operation?

options: EOM: electro-optic crystal whose refractive index changes with applied voltage; DC to GHz bandwidth; no frequency shift. AOM: travelling sound-wave diffraction grating; 1–100 MHz bandwidth; shifts frequency by the RF drive
AOM: electro-optic crystal with voltage-driven phase shift; DC to GHz bandwidth; no frequency shift. EOM: travelling sound-wave grating; 1–100 MHz bandwidth; shifts frequency by RF drive
Both EOM and AOM shift the laser frequency by the applied modulation frequency; only the bandwidth differs (GHz for EOM, MHz for AOM)
Both devices operate by Brewster-angle reflection from a birefringent crystal and differ only in the orientation of the optic axis
passive.explanation: AOM parameters: diffraction efficiency 70–90% into first order; rise time $\sim d/v_s$ where $d$ is the beam diameter and $v_s \sim 4$ km/s (sound speed in TeO$_2$); at $d = 0.5$ mm, rise time $\sim 125$ ns. Centre frequency 40–400 MHz (most common: 80 MHz for Rb). Double-pass AOM: beam passes through twice, frequency shift doubles ($2\times$ RF), and pointing stays fixed when RF is tuned. EOM usage: resonant EOMs have high sideband efficiency at a fixed modulation frequency; broadband EOMs allow arbitrary modulation. Half-wave voltage ($V_\pi$): $\sim 100$ V for bulk KDP at 780 nm; $\sim 10$ V for waveguide EOMs.

question: A photodiode + transimpedance amplifier (TIA) converts optical power to voltage. Which figure-of-merit relation correctly characterises the circuit?

options: Bandwidth $f_{-3\rm dB} \approx \sqrt{\rm GBW/(2\pi R_f C_d)}$; gain set by $R_f$; NEP $\propto \sqrt{4k_BT/R_f}/\mathcal{R}$; higher $R_f$ raises gain but lowers bandwidth
Bandwidth and gain are both proportional to $R_f$; raising $R_f$ improves both at once
Bandwidth is limited only by the photodiode's intrinsic capacitance and is independent of the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product
Responsivity is measured in V/W and is independent of the op-amp gain or feedback resistor
passive.explanation: TIA design: $R_f$ sets gain, $C_f$ is a small feedback capacitor for stability ($C_f \sim 1$ pF for $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$). Bandwidth: $f_{-3\rm dB} \approx \sqrt{\rm GBW}/(2\pi R_f C_d)^{1/2}$ where $C_d$ is diode capacitance. At $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$ and Si photodiode $C_d = 10$ pF with GBW = 200 MHz: bandwidth $\sim 100$ kHz. Trade-off: $R_f \uparrow$ → more gain, more Johnson noise, lower bandwidth. Si responsivity: $\mathcal{R} \approx 0.5$ A/W at 780 nm (η ≈ 80%); InGaAs: 1.0 A/W at 1560 nm. Balanced homodyne: subtract two PDs → common-mode noise rejected to $\sim 40$ dB below shot noise.

active.prompt: Match each detector/amplifier parameter to its definition and typical value for an 780 nm Si photodetector:

pairs.definition: Photocurrent per optical power: $\mathcal{R} = \eta e\lambda/(hc)$; for Si at 780 nm: $\sim 0.5$ A/W (quantum efficiency $\eta \approx 80\%$)
Output voltage per photocurrent: $V_{\rm out}/I_{\rm pd} = R_f$; typical values 1 k$\Omega$–1 M$\Omega$ depending on signal level and bandwidth needed
Minimum detectable optical power per $\sqrt{\rm Hz}$; dominated by Johnson noise of $R_f$: NEP $= \sqrt{4k_BT/R_f}/\mathcal{R}$; at $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$: $\sim 80$ pW/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$
Maximum optical input before op-amp saturates: $P_{\rm sat} = V_{\rm supply}/(R_f \mathcal{R})$; at $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$, $\pm 12$ V supply: $\sim 2.4$ mW

active.explanation: Practical tips: (1) Use a low-noise op-amp (e.g., OPA847, ADA4898) for high-bandwidth TIA. (2) Keep PCB traces from photodiode to TIA input as short as possible to minimise stray capacitance. (3) Shield the photodiode from stray light. (4) For single-photon detection: use APD in Geiger mode or SPAD — quantum efficiency 50–70% at 700–900 nm, dead time $\sim 50$ ns, dark count rate $\sim 100$ s$^{-1}$.

question: DDS and PLL are complementary RF synthesis tools for AOMs, EOMs, and ion-trap drives. Which combination correctly contrasts them?

options: DDS: arbitrary frequency with mHz resolution; fast (<1 μs) phase-coherent switching; limited to $< 500$ MHz. PLL: multiplies a stable reference up to GHz; lower phase noise; slower tuning
DDS: only integer multiples of a reference; coarse resolution; phase-incoherent switching. PLL: arbitrary frequency at mHz resolution; fast switching
DDS and PLL are functionally identical; the distinction is only the IC package and supply voltage
PLL is restricted to frequencies below 1 GHz; DDS routinely operates above 5 GHz with sub-Hz resolution
passive.explanation: DDS example: AD9959 (4-channel, 500 MSPS, 32-bit frequency word) → frequency resolution $\sim 0.1$ Hz at 100 MHz. Phase noise: $-130$ dBc/Hz at 1 kHz offset (limited by DAC quantisation). SFDR: $\sim 75$ dBc. Common usage: drive AOM at 80 MHz for detuning control, update frequency word every shot to sweep atom cloud. PLL example: HMC835 (25 MHz–3 GHz, fractional-N, $< -100$ dBc/Hz phase noise). Ion trap RF: 10–100 MHz at $\sim 10$ W — requires RF amplifier (Mini-Circuits ZHL) + helical resonator to transform 50 $\Omega$ to k$\Omega$ trap load. $V_{\rm rf} \sim 100{-}1000$ V$_{\rm pk-pk}$.

question: A helical resonator impedance-matches an RF amplifier (50 Ω) to the capacitive load of an ion-trap electrode. Which combination of design parameters correctly describes its operation?

options: Quality factor $Q = \omega L/R_{\rm coil}$; resonance $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$; voltage step-up $\sim Q/2$; bandwidth $\Delta\omega = \omega_0/Q$
It is a passive low-pass filter with no voltage gain; the only role is suppressing harmonics from the RF amplifier
Resonant frequency depends only on the trap electrode capacitance, not on the coil inductance
Voltage step-up scales as $1/Q$ — increasing coil resistance improves the gain
passive.explanation: Helical resonator design: $Q \sim 200{-}500$ for a well-designed coil (silver or copper on Teflon former, inside a copper can). Voltage gain: $V_{\rm trap}/V_{\rm in} \sim Q/2 \approx 100{-}250$, so 10 W input ($\sim 45$ V$_{\rm pk}$) → trap voltage $\sim 4000{-}11000$ V$_{\rm pk}$. Trap capacitance: 5–50 pF; determines coil inductance $L = 1/(\omega_0^2 C)$. Tuning: movable copper slug inside coil core. Monitoring: pickup coil near the main coil; capacitive divider on the electrode. Thermally mount the coil can to the vacuum chamber for drive-frequency temperature stability.
